verses.commentary: A man named Lazarus was sick. He was from Bethany, the village of Mary and her sister Martha. Location and family identified. Bethany near Jerusalem. The sisters introduced.
John assumes readers know the story. Mary characterized by worship.
No explicit request. They assume His love means action. Relationship implies response.
Death is not the end. Purpose: divine glory. The Son will be glorified. Death serves revelation.
Deep affection. What follows is not indifference but intentional delay.
Love delays. Strategic waiting. Greater glory through death and resurrection than immediate healing.
Dangerous territory. Recent stoning attempt (10:31). Courage required.
But Rabbi, a short while ago the Jews there tried to stone You, and yet You are going back? Disciples protest. Reasonable fear. Why return to danger? Self-preservation versus mission.
Metaphor. Daylight represents God's appointed time. Walking in His will brings safety. Divine timing protects. He operates within the Father's schedule.
Outside God's will brings peril. Timing is crucial.
Jesus will awaken him. Resurrection foreshadowed in language.
Sleep aids recovery. They miss the metaphor. Natural versus spiritual thinking.
Death, not rest. Communication gap.
No metaphor. Plain speech.
Delay served their faith. Greater miracle builds stronger belief. Pedagogical purpose.
Thomas commits to Jesus even unto death. Courageous but dark expectation.
Beyond resuscitation. Jewish belief: soul lingers three days, then departs. Lazarus is definitively dead. Decay begun.
Many Jerusalem Jews would come. Public miracle. Wide witness.
Shiva. Crowd present. Witnesses to grief and soon to glory.
Martha active, Mary contemplative. Both grieve differently.
Lord, if You had been here, my brother would not have died. Gentle accusation. Faith in His power. Regret at His absence. She believes He could have prevented death.
Even now: not too late. God will grant Jesus' requests. Belief in ongoing power.
Ambiguous timing. Future certain.
Final resurrection. Daniel 12:2. But she thinks distant future, not immediate.
Jesus is not just agent of resurrection but resurrection itself. Life inherent in Him. Believers transcend physical death.
Eternal life now. Question demands response. Personal faith required.

Yes, Lord, I believe that You are the Messiah, the Son of God, who is to come into the world. Creedal confession. Threefold title: Messiah, Son of God, Coming One. Martha's confession rivals Peter's (Matthew 16:16).
After she had said this, she went back and called her sister Mary aside. The Teacher is here, and is asking for you. She summons Mary. Teacher: personal title. Jesus requests her presence.
Devotion drives her. She goes to Jesus.
Jesus waits outside. Privacy for encounter.
Assumption: mourning ritual. Actually: meeting Messiah. Public witness to private encounter.
Same words as Martha. Shared grief, shared faith. Both sisters believe His presence prevents death.
Anger at death's ravages? Troubled: tarasso (ταράσσω), agitated. Jesus emotionally affected. Full humanity.
Where have you laid him? Come and see, Lord. Jesus asks location. They invite Him to the tomb. Parallel to 1:39, Come and see.
(ἐδάκρυσεν), shed tears. Shortest verse in Scripture. Profound theology. God weeps. Incarnation includes tears. Compassion, grief, full humanity displayed.
Correct reading. Affection genuine. Love made visible.
Logic: healing power should prevent death. They miss the greater plan. Resurrection exceeds prevention.
Tomb described. Cave sealed with stone. Burial place.
Take away the stone. But, Lord, by this time there is a bad odor, for he has been there four days. Command given. Martha objects. Decay expected. Stench of death. Four days confirms decomposition.
Promise recalled (verse 4). Faith precedes sight. Glory coming. Belief required.
Prayer begins. Thanksgiving precedes request. Already heard. Past tense. Prayer answered before spoken.
Father always hears. Public prayer for witness. Proof of divine mission. Sent by the Father.
Loud voice: authority. Specific name: if unnamed, all dead would rise. Power over death. Creation word speaks again.
Still bound. Alive but restricted. Jesus commands liberation. Community helps unwrap. Resurrection requires witnesses and helpers.
Eyewitnesses believe. Miracle validates. Many converted.
Miracle produces two responses: faith and fear. Some report to authorities. Hostility persists despite evidence.

Then the chief priests and the Pharisees called a meeting of the Sanhedrin. What are we accomplishing? Here is this man performing many signs. Emergency council. Admission of powerlessness. Signs accumulate. They cannot stop Him.
Fear of popular movement. Roman intervention threatened. Messiah brings trouble. Self-preservation over truth.
Dismissal of colleagues. High priest that year: John notes the rotation. Caiaphas in power AD 18-36.
Sacrifice one to save many. Utilitarian calculus. Political expediency. Unintentional prophecy.
Prophetic office speaks truth through wicked man. God uses even opponents. Jesus will indeed die for the nation.
Not just Jews. Scattered children: Gentiles to be gathered. Universal atonement. One people from many nations. Ephesians 2 foreshadowed.
Conspiracy formalized. Death sentence pronounced. Lazarus' resurrection seals Jesus' fate. Irony: giver of life condemned to death.
No longer public. Near wilderness. Final retreat before Passion. Time with disciples.
Pilgrims gather. Purification rites. The stage is set for final confrontation.
Searching. Will He risk appearing? Public curiosity.
Informants recruited. Manhunt. Authorities actively seeking to capture. The hour draws near.
